title: Python Decorators
summary: Decorators are functions that wrap another function to extend or modify its behavior without changing its source code.
common_mistakes: Forgetting to return the wrapper function
Not preserving the original function's metadata with functools.wraps
Confusing decorator arguments with the decorated function's arguments
practice_idea: Create a decorator that logs the name and execution time of any function it wraps.

title: Python Generators
summary: Generators are functions that produce a sequence of values lazily using the yield keyword, instead of returning them all at once.
common_mistakes: Assuming a generator can be iterated more than once
Materializing the whole generator into a list, losing the memory benefit
practice_idea: Write a generator that yields Fibonacci numbers up to a given limit without storing them all in memory.

title: List Comprehensions
summary: List comprehensions provide a concise syntax for creating lists by applying an expression to each item in an iterable.
common_mistakes: Writing overly complex nested comprehensions that hurt readability
Using comprehensions for side effects instead of building a list
practice_idea: Rewrite a for-loop that filters and transforms a list of numbers as a single list comprehension.

title: Exception Handling
summary: Exception handling lets a program respond to runtime errors using try, except, else, and finally blocks instead of crashing.
common_mistakes: Catching overly broad exceptions like bare except:
Using exceptions for normal control flow
practice_idea: Write a function that reads a file and raises a custom exception with a clear message if the file is missing.

title: Object-Oriented Inheritance
summary: Inheritance allows a class to reuse and extend the behavior of another class, forming a hierarchy of related types.
common_mistakes: Overusing inheritance where composition would be simpler
Creating deep or ambiguous inheritance hierarchies
practice_idea: Create a base class Animal with a speak method, then override it in two subclasses.

title: Async / Await
summary: Async and await enable writing asynchronous, non-blocking code that can run many I/O-bound tasks concurrently.
common_mistakes: Calling a coroutine without awaiting it
Blocking the event loop with synchronous code
Assuming async code runs in parallel on multiple CPU cores
practice_idea: Write two async functions that fetch mock data concurrently using asyncio.gather.
